channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed - www.gamedeveloper.com
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for www.gamedeveloper.com

item.title: Fortnite returns to the App Store everywhere except Australia
item.content:encoded:
The ongoing dispute between Epic Games and Apple has led to Fortnite returning to the App Store worldwide. Except for Australia, that is.

Per a press release shared by Epic today, the company emphasized that this decision came to be after Apple told the U.S. Supreme Court that "regulators around the world are watching this case to determine what commission rate Apple may charge on covered purchases in huge markets outside the United States."

The news was shared alongside stated expectations that US federal courts will require Apple to disclose its fee structure. According to Epic, Apple "knows the U.S. federal court will force it to be transparent" about how it charges fees in the App Store.

As such, Fortnite's return to the App Store reflects Epic's confidence that, "once Apple is forced to show its costs, governments around the world will not allow Apple junk fees to stand."

Elsewhere in the statement, Epic notes it will "continue to challenge Apple's anticompetitive App Store," pertaining to current policies banning alternative app stores and payment systems.

Related:CI Games and Epic Games terminate Lords of the Fallen II publishing deal

"Time and time again Apple has evaded the laws with scare screens, fees, and onerous requirements," Epic continues. "It's time for regulators to truly enforce the laws so developers and consumers around the world can benefit from an open and fair mobile app ecosystem."

Fortnite's legal battle with Apple continues in Australia

Fortnite's lengthy hiatus on the App Store started to fall off across different regions back in 2025. In May, it returned to the US App Store after a five-year absence. Then, in August, Epic shared the news that both Fortnite and the Epic Games Store will become available on iOS in Australia.

At the time, Epic's announcement read: "An Australian court just found that Apple and Google abuse their control over app distribution and in-app payments to limit competition. There are 2,000+ pages of findings that we'll need to dig into to fully understand the details. This is a win for developers and consumers in Australia."

Yet, as part of today's news, Fortnite is still not back on the Australian App Store. According to Epic, while it won the court case against Apple—with the court finding many of Apple's developer terms unlawful—Epic claims that Apple continues to enforce those terms.

"Epic is now asking the Court to bring Apple's unlawful conduct to an end and to make orders that will benefit all app developers and iOS users. Epic can't return under an illegal payment arrangement with Apple, so unless Apple agrees to adopt lawful payment terms in the interim, we must wait for a Court decision."

Related:Sony increasing the price of PlayStation Plus for new customers

The Fortnite maker is having an eventful 2026, to say the least. In March, Epic and Google settled a different long-lasting dispute, marking the return of Fortnite on the Google Play store.

During the announcement that the company would be laying off over 1,000 employees in March, Epic CEO Tim Sweeney suggested that Fortnite's protracted absence from mobile platforms took its toll.

"We're only in the early stages of returning to mobile and optimizing Fortnite for the world's billions of smartphones; and in being the industry's vanguard we have taken a lot of bullets in a battle which is only in the early days of paying off for ourselves and all developers," wrote Sweeney at the time.

About the Author

Contributing Editor, News, GameDeveloper.com

Diego Nicolás Argüello is a freelance journalist and critic from Argentina. Video games helped him to learn English, so now he covers them for places like The New York Times, NPR, Rolling Stone, and more. He also runs Into the Spine, a site dedicated to fostering and supporting new writers, and co-hosted Turnabout Breakdown, a podcast about the Ace Attorney series. He’s most likely playing a rhythm game as you read this.



https://www.gamedeveloper.com/business/fortnite-returns-to-the-app-store-everywhere-except-australia
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot
